posts.content:
Hot take. Come at me.

Excel is still the most important tool for analysts in 2026 — more important than Python, Power BI, or any AI tool — because nothing else gives you the combination of flexibility, speed, and auditability in a single tool that everyone on your team already has.

A) Agree. Excel is irreplaceable.
B) Agree for now. Will change.
C) Disagree. Python wins now.
D) The question itself is wrong.

Drop your honest take and tell me what you are actually seeing in your workplace right now 👇

Monday check-in.

What kind of Excel day was it?

A) Most capable in the room.
B) Solid day. Handled everything.
C) Humbled. Probably needed it.
D) 4 PM file. Still in it.

Drop the one thing that defined your Excel Monday 👇

What is the Excel habit you know is wrong but you are too far into the project to fix now?

A) Full column references like =SUM(A:A) scattered through the entire file
B) A nested IF so deep you could not explain it to anyone including yourself
C) A manual step in a process that should have been automated six months ago
D) Twelve copies of the same file all named some version of FINAL

Vote and confess below. No judgment from this community. We have all been here.

Your XLOOKUP keeps returning "Not Found" even though the value definitely exists in the table.

This is almost never the formula. It is almost always the data.

Problem 1 — Invisible spaces
The value in your lookup column has a leading or trailing space you cannot see.
Fix: =XLOOKUP(TRIM(A2), TRIM(lookup_range), return_range, "Not Found")

Problem 2 — Numbers stored as text
Your ID column looks like numbers but Excel is treating them as text.
Fix: =XLOOKUP(VALUE(A2), lookup_range, return_range, "Not Found")

Problem 3 — Mixed formatting
One column is formatted as General, the other as Text. They look identical. Excel sees them as different types.
Fix: Select both columns → Format Cells → set both to the same format → Ctrl+H → Find a space → Replace with nothing → OK

The combination that catches 90% of broken lookups:
=XLOOKUP(TRIM(VALUE(A2)), lookup_range, return_range, "Not Found")

Drop the lookup error that has been driving you insane. I will diagnose it in the comments.

→ Full XLOOKUP edge case breakdown in Excel Flow Edition #28
https://rileystream.substack.com/p/the-four-languages-of-modern-excel

Real scenario from this week:

"My dashboard runs fine on my machine but takes three minutes to open on everyone else's. My manager is asking why the report is so slow."

Diagnosis in order:

Step 1 — Check your last used cell
Press Ctrl+End. If Excel jumps to row 50,000 but your data only goes to row 500, phantom formatting is forcing Excel to evaluate empty cells. Select everything below your real data, delete it, and save.

Step 2 — Check for full column references
=VLOOKUP(A2, B:D, 2, FALSE) forces Excel to search one million cells. Replace with Table references or a defined range.

Step 3 — Check conditional formatting scope
Home → Conditional Formatting → Manage Rules. Rules applied to $A:$A instead of $A$2:$A$500 are processing a million cells on every recalculation. Reapply to the exact range you need.

Step 4 — Remove broken named ranges
Ctrl+F3 opens Name Manager. Delete anything showing #REF! errors.

For the multi-person refresh problem: move the file to SharePoint or OneDrive and have everyone open it from there rather than downloading a local copy. That alone typically cuts load time by half.

Drop your slowest file situation below. Tell me what it does and roughly how many rows and I will tell you exactly what to check first.

Unpopular Excel opinion that I want this group to either confirm or destroy.

The most dangerous person in any office is not the one who knows the least Excel.

It is the one who knows just enough to be confidently wrong.

A) Agree. Cleaned up that damage.
B) Disagree. Confidence delivers.
C) Depends on documentation.
D) I have been this person.

Drop the story. No names required. 👇

You have a date column that Excel refuses to recognize as actual dates. They look right. Nothing works.

Scenario 1 — Dates formatted as YYYY-MM-DD
Data → Text to Columns → Next → Next → Date: YMD → Finish

Scenario 2 — Dates formatted as DD/MM/YYYY
Same steps but choose DMY instead of YMD

Scenario 3 — Dates with timestamps like "2026-06-01 14:30:00"
=DATEVALUE(LEFT(A2,10))
Strips the time and converts the date portion to a real Excel date value.

Scenario 4 — Nothing above works
=DATE(MID(A2,1,4), MID(A2,6,2), MID(A2,9,2))
Manually extracts year, month, and day from the text string and builds a real date from scratch. Works regardless of how broken the original formatting is.

Format the resulting column as Date and you are done.

Drop your worst date formatting nightmare. If it is not one of these four I will build the fix in the comments.

→ Full data cleaning walkthrough in Excel Flow Edition #25
https://rileystream.substack.com/p/mynda-treacy-the-1-excel-mistake

Real scenario:

"I need to calculate working days between two dates excluding weekends AND our company holidays. NETWORKDAYS handles weekends but I cannot figure out how to add holidays."

NETWORKDAYS already does this. Most people miss the third argument.

=NETWORKDAYS(start_date, end_date, holidays_range)

Put your holiday dates in a column, name the range Holidays, then:
=NETWORKDAYS(A2, B2, Holidays)

Now it excludes weekends AND every date on your list automatically. When holidays change next year you update the list and every formula updates itself.

For custom work weeks (four-day weeks, Sunday-Thursday schedules):
=NETWORKDAYS.INTL(A2, B2, "0000011", Holidays)

The string "0000011" tells Excel which days are weekends. Each position represents Mon through Sun. 1 means weekend, 0 means workday. "0000011" is standard Saturday-Sunday. "0000001" is Sunday only (common in Middle East schedules).

Drop your date calculation scenario below. If NETWORKDAYS is not solving it I will write the formula that does.

Real question. Honest answers only.

How confident are you in your Excel skills when someone is watching versus when you are alone?

A) Exactly the same — I work the same way regardless
B) Slightly more careful but equally capable

You need to find duplicates in 10,000 rows and you are not doing it manually.

Just highlight them:
Select column → Conditional Formatting → Highlight Cell Rules → Duplicate Values. Four clicks. Every duplicate turns red.

Flag them with a formula:
=IF(COUNTIF($A$2:$A$10000, A2)>1, "Duplicate", "Unique")
Filter by "Duplicate" to see only those rows.

Remove them completely:
Select data → Data → Remove Duplicates → choose columns to compare. Excel deletes duplicates and tells you how many it removed.

Keep only unique values dynamically:
=UNIQUE(A2:A10000)
Spills a live list of every unique value. Updates automatically when source data changes.

Conditional Formatting is fastest for a one-time visual check. UNIQUE is most powerful for ongoing use.

Drop your duplicate problem and I will tell you which approach fits it.

→ Dynamic array functions in Excel Flow Edition #27
https://substack.com/@rileystream/p-197635354

posts.content:
The file structure question that divides every Excel professional.

When you build a workbook for someone else how many sheets do you use?

A) One sheet for everything.
B) Separate sheets. Always.
C) As many as the logic requires.
D) README tab. Non-negotiable.

Drop your exact structure and why. Bonus points if you changed your approach after learning it the hard way 👇

Real scenario:

"I built a dashboard for my team. It looks great. Nobody uses it. They still email me for the numbers every morning."

This is a dashboard adoption problem not a technical problem. Here is what causes it and how to fix each one:

Cause 1 — They do not know it exists or where to find it
Pin the link in Teams or Slack. Send one email introducing it. Show it in a meeting for two minutes.

Cause 2 — It requires them to refresh or do something technical
Set the data to refresh automatically. Or refresh it yourself each morning before they start their day.

Cause 3 — It answers the question you think they have not the one they actually have
Ask three people on your team what number they check first every morning. Build the dashboard around those three numbers specifically.

Cause 4 — It requires interpretation they are not confident making
Add a one-sentence plain English summary at the top. "Revenue is up 8% week over week driven by the Enterprise segment." They read the summary and go deeper if they want to.

A dashboard nobody uses is not a dashboard. Build what people will actually open first.

Drop your dashboard adoption story below. If you solved this problem I want to know exactly what worked.

The Excel vs AI debate. My actual take after talking to analysts, FP&A leads, and data teams every week for a year:

AI is not replacing Excel. It is eliminating the 30% of Excel work that was always just data cleaning and reformatting. The 70% that is judgment, modeling, and communication is becoming more valuable not less.

A) Agree. AI for grunt work.
B) Disagree. AI's coming faster.
C) Too early. Ask in 18 months.
D) I build. I don't overthink it.

Drop what you are actually seeing in your workflow right now 👇

A) Whether their data is in proper Tables or just raw ranges
B) How they name their sheets and structure the file
C) The formulas they reach for and how readable they are
D) Whether they use keyboard shortcuts or the mouse for everything

Vote and drop the first thing you check when you open someone else's file.

Your SUMIFS keeps returning zero even though the numbers are definitely there.

Cause 1 — Date criteria not formatted correctly
=SUMIFS(Amount, Date, ">=01/06/2026") will always return zero. Excel does not parse dates in strings this way.
Fix: =SUMIFS(Amount, Date, ">="&DATE(2026,6,1))

Cause 2 — Text criteria has invisible spaces
Your criteria column has " West " instead of "West".
Fix: TRIM your criteria range or use a helper column.

Cause 3 — Numbers stored as text
Your criteria column contains numbers formatted as text. SUMIFS will not match numeric criteria against them.
Fix: Reformat the column as numbers or wrap criteria in VALUE().

Cause 4 — Mismatched range sizes
=SUMIFS(A2:A100, B2:B101, "West") — mismatched ranges return zero silently. Every range must cover exactly the same number of rows.

Fastest diagnostic: Replace SUMIFS with COUNTIFS using the same criteria. If COUNTIFS also returns zero you know the matching is broken not the summing.

Drop your SUMIFS returning zero. Tell me what you are trying to match and I will find the problem in the comments.

→ Advanced SUMIFS patterns in Excel Flow Edition #26
https://rileystream.substack.com/p/carl-seidman-why-excel-skills-dont

Real scenario:

"I need each salesperson's total revenue, their rank on the team, and whether they are above or below the team average. I have the revenue but the rank and comparison are manual right now."

Three formulas, one for each:

Rank:
=RANK(B2, $B$2:$B$20, 0)
0 means highest value = rank 1. Change to 1 for ascending. Use RANK.AVG for ties.

Above or below average:
=IF(B2 > AVERAGE($B$2:$B$20), "Above", "Below")

Make it informative:
=IF(B2>AVERAGE($B$2:$B$20), "+"&TEXT(B2-AVERAGE($B$2:$B$20),"$#,##0")&" above avg", TEXT(AVERAGE($B$2:$B$20)-B2,"$#,##0")&" below avg")

This gives you "+$12,400 above avg" or "$3,200 below avg" instead of just a label. Add conditional formatting to highlight above-average rows green and you have a complete report in about ten minutes.

Drop the reporting formula you have been building manually. Tell me what the output needs to look like and I will write it.

End of week and I want a real answer.

The Excel skill that made the single biggest difference in your career. Not the flashiest one. The one that actually compounded.

A) Power Query — automating data cleaning changed everything permanently
B) Pivot tables — being able to slice any dataset instantly changed how I work
C) Financial modeling discipline — structure, documentation, auditability
D) Keyboard fluency — being twice as fast as everyone else in the room

Vote and tell me when you learned it and what actually changed after.

You are building the same report every week and it takes two hours. Here is how to cut that to fifteen minutes.

Step 1 — List every manual step
Every step. Data download, paste, clean, format, calculate, format again, copy to template. Write them all out.

Step 2 — Classify each one
A = happens the same way every time
B = changes based on the data
C = requires judgment

Step 3 — Automate everything marked A
Every step that is identical every week is a Power Query transformation. Build it once. It runs itself.

Step 4 — Systematize everything marked B
These are formulas that reference whatever Power Query prepared. Build once, update automatically.

Step 5 — Protect your time for everything marked C
These are the decisions only you can make. Eliminate A and B and this is all you do.

Most two-hour weekly reports become fifteen minutes of clicking Refresh and making judgment calls. The rest was always automatable.

Drop the weekly report that is eating your time. Tell me what it does and I will tell you which steps Power Query eliminates.

→ Full Power Query automation walkthrough in Excel Flow Edition #25
https://rileystream.substack.com/p/mynda-treacy-the-1-excel-mistake

Real scenario:

"I have 30,000 rows of transaction data. I need to flag anything more than three standard deviations from the mean as an outlier. I have been doing this manually."

One formula:
=IF(ABS(A2-AVERAGE($A$2:$A$30000))>3*STDEV($A$2:$A$30000),"Outlier","Normal")

How it works: AVERAGE calculates the mean. STDEV calculates the standard deviation. Multiply STDEV by 3 to get the three-sigma boundary. ABS makes it work for both high and low outliers. IF flags anything beyond that boundary.

Dynamic array version that fills all 30,000 rows from a single cell:
=IF(ABS(A2:A30000-AVERAGE(A2:A30000))>3*STDEV(A2:A30000),"Outlier","Normal")

Enter it once. It spills automatically.

Drop your outlier detection problem. If you need to detect outliers within groups (outliers per product category, per region, per month) I will show you the AVERAGEIF and STDEV approach for that.

B) A fitness, nutrition, or habit tracking spreadsheet

Your personal budget spreadsheet is overcomplicated and underused. Here is the version that actually works.

Five columns you actually need:
Date | Category | Description | Amount | Running Total

That is it. Everything else is noise until you have been consistent for three months.

Running total: =SUM($D$2:D2) — drag down and it updates automatically.

Monthly summary: =SUMIFS(D:D, A:A, ">="&DATE(2026,6,1), A:A, "<="&EOMONTH(DATE(2026,6,1),0))

Category breakdown: A pivot table with Category as rows and Amount as values. Refresh once a week.

The one rule: Enter every transaction the same day it happens. Two minutes a day beats a two-hour Sunday catch-up. The spreadsheet only works if the data is current.

Drop the personal finance Excel feature you built and actually use versus the one you built and abandoned.

Real scenario:

"I have 12 months of bank statements as PDFs. I do not want to type every transaction in manually."

Option 1 — CSV export from your bank
Most banks have a download option under transaction history. CSV format opens directly in Excel. Check this first before anything else.

Option 2 — PDF with real table data
Adobe Acrobat or any PDF reader → Export → Spreadsheet → Excel. Works when the PDF contains actual table data not scanned images.

Option 3 — Power Query PDF connector
Data → Get Data → From File → From PDF. Power Query detects tables in the PDF and imports them. Works on most bank statement formats.

Option 4 — Scanned image PDF
Copy all text (Ctrl+A, Ctrl+C), paste into Excel, use Flash Fill (Ctrl+E) to extract dates, amounts, and descriptions from the raw text. Takes about 15 minutes per month's statement.

The CSV export from your bank is always the fastest path if it exists.

Drop your bank statement format. Tell me what your bank offers and I will give you the exact path.

You are still manually copying data between sheets when a formula handles it automatically.

Pull one value from another sheet:
=Sheet2!B5

Pull a value from a sheet whose name is in a cell:
=INDIRECT("'"&A2&"'!B5")
Where A2 contains the sheet name. Change one cell to pull from a different sheet.

Pull and summarize from another sheet:
=SUMIF(Sheet2!A:A, "West", Sheet2!B:B)

Consolidate 12 monthly sheets in one formula:
=SUM(January:December!B47)
This 3D reference sums cell B47 across every sheet between January and December. Works with SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MIN, MAX.

Consolidate files from an entire folder:
Power Query → Get Data → From File → From Folder → Combine. Every file in the folder combined into one table. Click Refresh to update.

Drop the between-sheet copy-paste you are still doing manually and I will write the formula that eliminates it.

→ Multi-sheet consolidation in Excel Flow Edition #27
https://substack.com/@rileystream/p-197635354

Real scenario:

"I need a drop-down where the options change based on what someone picked in the previous drop-down. If they pick North the next drop-down should only show Northern cities."

This is a dependent drop-down. Here is the clean build:

Step 1 — Set up your lists
Create a table with one column per region. Under North list Northern cities. Under South list Southern cities.

Step 2 — Name each list
Select your North city list → Name Box (top left) → type North → Enter. Repeat for each region. The name must match the region option exactly including capitalization.

Step 3 — First drop-down
Select the region cell → Data → Data Validation → List → type your regions: North,South,East,West

Step 4 — Second drop-down
Select the city cell → Data → Data Validation → List → Source: =INDIRECT(A2)
Where A2 is your region cell. INDIRECT converts the text "North" into the named range called North.

When someone picks North the city drop-down shows only Northern cities. Picks South and it shows only Southern cities automatically.

The one limitation: adding new cities requires updating the named range for that region. Use Tables as your source lists to make named ranges dynamic and self-expanding.

Drop your drop-down dependency problem. If you need more than two levels I will show you how to handle the additional layers.
